mnemonics: PAPA's Genes: Public, Accessible, Protected, Accessible. (Public members are accessible to everyone, Protected members are accessible to the class and its children, like genes passed to offspring)
IS-A Test: A 'Car' IS-A 'Vehicle'. This simple phrase helps remember the core 'is-a' relationship that defines inheritance.
keyPoints: Inheritance allows a new class (derived/child class) to acquire the properties and behaviors of an existing class (base/parent class), promoting code reusability.
The 'protected' access specifier is unique to inheritance; it makes members accessible within the class itself and in any class that inherits from it, but not to the outside world.
C++ supports different types of inheritance: public, protected, and private, which determine the accessibility of the inherited base class members in the derived class.
commonMistakes: Forgetting that constructors and destructors are not inherited. The derived class must define its own, often calling the base class constructor explicitly.

Confusing 'private' and 'protected' members. A common error is trying to access a base class's private members directly from the derived class, which is not allowed. Only public and protected members are accessible.
Creating overly deep or complex inheritance hierarchies (the 'god object' or 'fragile base class' problem) which can make the code difficult to maintain and understand.
